stem: A male infant is born at 40+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for placental abruption after a sentinel maternal collapse 25 minutes before delivery; the mother presented to a regional birthing unit with sudden severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding and was transferred urgently to theatre when the CTG showed prolonged bradycardia at 60 bpm. Estimated time of insult to delivery is 22 minutes. APGARs were 1 at 1 minute, 3 at 5 minutes and 5 at 10 minutes. Cord arterial gas shows pH 6.92, base deficit 18 mmol/L, and lactate 11 mmol/L. He required positive pressure ventilation from birth, intubation at 4 minutes for ongoing bradycardia, and three doses of intratracheal then intravenous adrenaline 10 microg/kg via emergency umbilical venous catheter before return of spontaneous circulation at 8 minutes. He is now 45 minutes old, intubated on conventional ventilation in the NICU at a tertiary centre with active servo-controlled cooling preparations underway, including a cooling mattress and oesophageal probe. He has reduced spontaneous activity with intermittent generalised tonic posturing of the limbs lasting around 20 seconds, sluggish pupils that are 3 mm and equally reactive, a weak suck and absent gag, and an exaggerated Moro reflex with prolonged opening. His axillary temperature on arrival was 36.4 deg C and the team has held active warming, allowing passive cooling, while preparing servo-controlled cooling. There is no congenital anomaly, no major birth trauma, and no clinical evidence of bleeding. He weighs 3540 g and has no surgical contraindication. Imaging is pending. The treating consultant must decide on definitive neuroprotective therapy in the next 15 minutes, well within the 6 hour therapeutic window from birth.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Permit passive cooling to 35.5 deg C until 24 hours of age then rewarm over 12 hours
options.rationale: Passive cooling to 35.5 deg C does not provide the target therapeutic range; servo-controlled cooling to 33 to 34 deg C is required to achieve neuroprotection, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

options.text: Maintain strict normothermia 36.5-37.5 deg C and commence prophylactic phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading dose
options.rationale: Normothermia is not the goal in confirmed moderate or severe HIE within 6 hours of birth; whole-body therapeutic hypothermia is the evidence-based neuroprotective therapy, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

options.text: Whole-body therapeutic hypothermia 33-34 deg C for 72 hours with aEEG, rewarm 12 hours, phenobarbitone for seizures
options.rationale: Term or near-term infants (at least 35 weeks gestation) within 6 hours of birth with evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia and moderate to severe encephalopathy (Sarnat 2 or 3) meet criteria for whole-body cooling to 33 to 34 deg C for 72 hours, with rewarming at 0.5 deg C per hour, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for clinical seizures, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Initiate selective head cooling with cap to scalp temperature 28 deg C for 48 hours and rapid rewarming
options.rationale: Selective head cooling is an alternative technique but the duration is 72 hours not 48, and Australian and New Zealand units predominantly use whole-body cooling because of monitoring convenience and equivalent outcomes, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

options.text: Delay cooling for 12 hours pending MRI brain, commence hypothermia only if injury confirmed
options.rationale: Cooling must start within 6 hours of birth to achieve neuroprotective effect; waiting for MRI misses the therapeutic window and is contraindicated, per Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

stem: A male infant is born at 40+3 weeks after a labour complicated by thick meconium-stained liquor and a non-reassuring CTG with late decelerations in the second stage. Birthweight is 3680 g. APGARs were 4 at 1 minute and 7 at 5 minutes. He required brief positive pressure ventilation in the delivery room and is now 6 hours old in the level 3 NICU at a tertiary perinatal centre, on conventional ventilation with FiO2 0.85, peak inspiratory pressure 24 cm H2O, PEEP 6 cm H2O, mean airway pressure 14 cm H2O, and an oxygenation index of 22 calculated from an arterial blood gas with PaO2 50 mmHg. Preductal SpO2 measured on the right hand is 92 percent and postductal SpO2 on the right foot is 78 percent, a sustained difference of 14 percent. Chest radiograph shows patchy coarse infiltrates with hyperinflation consistent with meconium aspiration. Urgent echocardiography by the neonatal cardiologist shows right-to-left shunting across the patent ductus arteriosus, tricuspid regurgitation with an estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 70 mmHg, septal flattening at end-systole, and structurally normal cardiac anatomy with normal pulmonary venous return on detailed views. Surfactant 100 mg/kg has already been given via the endotracheal tube. He has a normal capillary refill of 2 seconds on milrinone 0.5 microg/kg/min and dobutamine 5 microg/kg/min, with a mean arterial pressure of 42 mmHg. There is no pneumothorax on transillumination or repeat CXR.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: Prostaglandin E1 is used to maintain ductal patency in duct-dependent CHD; structurally normal heart with PPHN does not benefit, and PGE1 can worsen pulmonary congestion, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

options.text: Inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm via ventilator and reassess oxygenation index after 30-60 min
options.rationale: PPHN with an oxygenation index above 20 in a term infant after optimisation of ventilation and surfactant warrants inhaled nitric oxide at a starting dose of 20 ppm; if response is inadequate or OI rises despite iNO, ECMO referral should be considered, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn and BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.

options.text: Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation immediately and avoid pulmonary vasodilators today
options.rationale: HFOV is a useful adjunct to recruit atelectatic lung in MAS and may be combined with iNO, but it does not replace pulmonary vasodilator therapy in established PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

options.text: Refer immediately for emergency ECMO without commencing iNO or HFOV trial
options.rationale: ECMO is reserved for refractory PPHN with OI persistently above 25 to 40 despite iNO and HFOV; bypassing iNO at OI 22 is premature, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

options.text: Commence oral sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg every 6 hours as primary pulmonary vasodilator therapy
options.rationale: Oral sildenafil is used as adjunctive or weaning therapy after iNO; it is not first-line in acute severe PPHN where rapid pulmonary vasodilatation with iNO is required, per BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.

stem: A term male infant born at 39+6 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth to a 27-year-old G1P0 woman is now 36 hours old on the postnatal ward of a tertiary maternity hospital. APGARs were 9 and 9 and the perinatal course was uncomplicated with no maternal infection, no chorioamnionitis, no prolonged ROM, no instrumental delivery, no resuscitation and a normal cord gas (arterial pH 7.32, base deficit 4 mmol/L). Birthweight was 3380 g. He has been breastfeeding well with audible swallows, with normal heelprick BSLs of 3.0 to 4.5 mmol/L throughout, and has had three wet nappies and meconium passed at 8 hours. There is no family history of consanguinity, neonatal seizures or sudden infant death. At 36 hours he develops sudden onset rhythmic jerking of the right arm and right leg lasting around 90 seconds, with lip smacking and a brief apnoea with desaturation to 88 percent, followed by a similar focal motor seizure 20 minutes later. He is now post-ictal with reduced tone in the right limbs but rouseable to handling. Examination reveals no dysmorphic features, no rash, no neurocutaneous stigmata (no cafe-au-lait or hypopigmented macules under Wood's lamp), and a normal anterior fontanelle. He has not been cooled and has no risk factors for HIE. Sodium is 138 mmol/L, ionised calcium 1.25 mmol/L, magnesium 0.8 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L, ammonia 60 micromol/L, blood gas pH 7.36 with normal lactate, and a sepsis screen including blood culture, FBC, CRP and lumbar puncture is in progress. EEG and cranial ultrasound are being arranged.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg and observe for cessation of seizure activity
options.rationale: Diazepam is not the first-line agent in neonatal seizures and rectal administration is unreliable; phenobarbitone IV is preferred for sustained levels and proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Give intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg empirically for presumed hypocalcaemic seizure
options.rationale: The ionised calcium of 1.25 mmol/L is within the normal range; empirical calcium is not indicated and can cause arrhythmia if extravasated, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Commence pyridoxine 100 mg IV and withhold antiepileptic therapy until response is assessed
options.rationale: Pyridoxine is reserved for refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to standard antiepileptics; it should not delay first-line phenobarbitone, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg, blood/urine for metabolic and infection screen, LP, urgent MRI
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for confirmed neonatal seizures in Australian protocols; concurrently the cause must be sought (sepsis, metabolic, intracranial bleed or infarct, structural). MRI is preferred for parenchymal injury and stroke detection, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures and Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology.

options.text: Initiate therapeutic hypothermia at 33.5 deg C for 72 hours as initial seizure therapy
options.rationale: This baby does not meet cooling criteria; he had no perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, normal cord gas and normal APGARs, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

stem: A male infant is born at 32+0 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 28-year-old G1P0 woman with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes 5 days before delivery. She was admitted for monitoring, received prophylactic erythromycin and a course of antenatal corticosteroids; specifically, two doses of antenatal betamethasone with the second dose 48 hours before delivery, and a single dose of magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection commenced 4 hours before delivery. There was no maternal fever, the CRP remained normal, and the white cell count was 11 x10^9/L pre-delivery. APGARs were 7 and 8 with brief CPAP via T-piece in the delivery room. Birthweight is 1640 g (50th centile for gestation). He is transferred to the level 3 NICU at 10 minutes of age. From birth he has had moderate work of breathing with intercostal recession, mild grunting, and SpO2 of 89 percent in room air. He is now on nasal CPAP 7 cm H2O with FiO2 0.35 delivered via short binasal prongs, has mild grunting that is gradually improving, and is haemodynamically stable with a mean BP of 38 mmHg. The team prepares to administer surfactant in the next 30 minutes. There is no antenatal evidence of infection. He has not yet had a chest radiograph.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surfactant administration strategy in this infant?

options.text: Continue CPAP without surfactant unless FiO2 rises above 0.6 or work of breathing worsens
options.rationale: Threshold for surfactant in preterm RDS in many AU units is sustained FiO2 above 0.3 (under 28 weeks) or above 0.3 to 0.4 (28 to 32 weeks); ignoring an FiO2 of 0.35 in a 32-week infant with grunting risks delayed treatment, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Intubate, give surfactant, and continue mechanical ventilation for at least the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Prolonged ventilation after surfactant increases the risk of bronchopulmonary dysplasia in a haemodynamically stable preterm infant who is breathing spontaneously; less invasive strategies are preferred, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Repeat a second course of antenatal corticosteroid postnatally as IM betamethasone to the infant to enhance lung maturation
options.rationale: Postnatal corticosteroids in this context are inappropriate; antenatal corticosteroids must be given to the mother before delivery, and postnatal dexamethasone is reserved for ventilator-dependent infants at risk of BPD, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Give nebulised surfactant via the CPAP circuit at 200 mg/kg over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised surfactant remains investigational and is not currently recommended outside trials in Australia; tracheal delivery is required for proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

options.text: Continue CPAP and give surfactant via thin catheter using LISA technique with spontaneous breathing
options.rationale: In a preterm infant breathing spontaneously on CPAP, LISA (also called MIST in some AU centres) allows surfactant administration via a thin tracheal catheter while CPAP and spontaneous breathing continue, and reduces the rate of mechanical ventilation and BPD compared with INSURE, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration and Cochrane: LISA versus INSURE.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to a paediatric cardiology outpatient clinic after two episodes of exertional syncope. The first occurred while sprinting at school athletics, with a brief loss of consciousness during which her friends say her limbs jerked for around 10 seconds before she came round confused for 30 seconds. The second was while jumping in a pool 6 weeks later. There is no history of typical post-ictal phase, tongue biting, or incontinence. A maternal uncle died suddenly in his 20s while swimming; the family was told it was drowning. She takes no regular medications and has never used a QT-prolonging drug. Examination is unremarkable with normal heart sounds, no murmur, and normal blood pressure. A resting 12-lead ECG at the clinic is shown to a paediatric electrophysiologist. Manual measurement and Bazett correction across multiple leads in the same patient give a QTc of 510 ms, with broad-based notched T-waves in the precordial leads. She has not previously had any cardiac investigations or family screening, she takes no over-the-counter or herbal medications, and her resting heart rate today is 58 with no postural drop. There is no history of hearing impairment in the family.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassurance, electrolyte replacement, and follow-up ECG in 12 months
options.rationale: QTc above 480 ms with exertional syncope and a suggestive family history meets diagnostic criteria for LQTS; reassurance alone is inappropriate, per Heart Rhythm Society and AHA expert consensus.

options.text: Non-selective beta-blocker propranolol, exercise restriction, cascade ECG screening of relatives
options.rationale: LQTS with syncope is high-risk; non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol or nadolol) reduce events, exercise restriction is recommended particularly for swimming in LQT1, and first-degree relatives need cascade screening with ECG +/- genetic testing, per AHA/HRS consensus and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily for QT shortening
options.rationale: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-selective beta-blockers without QT-prolonging effect (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred, per AMH and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Immediate implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) implantation as first-line therapy
options.rationale: ICD is reserved for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification, per AHA/HRS consensus.

options.text: Commence levetiracetam after referral to a neurologist for presumed seizures
options.rationale: Although hypoxic limb jerking can mimic seizures, the exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point firmly to LQTS not epilepsy, per BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome.

stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed at a regional hospital after 10 days of migratory polyarthritis affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists in sequence over the preceding week. Each joint has been hot, painful, and swollen for 2 to 3 days before migrating to another joint, with dramatic and rapid resolution. She also had a sore throat 4 weeks ago that resolved without antibiotic treatment, and a throat swab taken at that time was not collected. She has no prior diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever and no known rheumatic heart disease on previous school-based screening echocardiograms performed at age 7. She has had no rash and no subcutaneous nodules detected on careful palpation of extensor surfaces. She has not had a documented fever during her current presentation, though her mother reports a febrile illness during the recent pharyngitis. There is no carditis clinically and a transthoracic echocardiogram performed today shows a structurally normal heart with no valvular regurgitation or pericardial effusion. ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with a PR interval of 220 ms (above the age-related upper limit). Anti-streptolysin O titre is 540 IU/mL and anti-DNase B is 380 U/mL, both elevated above the laboratory reference range. C-reactive protein is 60 mg/L, ESR is 65 mm/h, and a full blood count is otherwise unremarkable. The treating team is applying the 2020 RHDAustralia guideline using the criteria for high-risk populations.
lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether this presentation meets the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria for an initial episode of acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population?

options.text: Does not meet criteria because she is afebrile and fever is mandatory for diagnosis
options.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with evidence of recent GAS infection, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because echocardiogram is normal and subclinical carditis is required
options.rationale: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence of carditis does not exclude ARF when other majors and minors are present, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets definite initial ARF: polyarthritis (major in high-risk), prolonged PR and elevated APRs (minors), plus recent GAS
options.rationale: Under the 2020 RHDAustralia high-risk criteria, polyarthritis is a major; she has 1 major (polyarthritis) plus 3 minors (prolonged PR for age, raised CRP and ESR) plus serological evidence of recent GAS (raised ASOT and anti-DNase B), satisfying the 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence threshold for definite initial ARF, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets criteria only as probable ARF because monoarthritis is required in high-risk populations
options.rationale: In high-risk populations, monoarthritis or polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major and yields definite, not probable, ARF when criteria are otherwise met, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because anti-DNase B is non-specific and only ASOT counts as GAS evidence
options.rationale: Insufficient information is incorrect: the diagnostic criteria have been explicitly addressed (1 major + 2 minors with GAS evidence) which suffices for a definite initial ARF diagnosis in a high-risk population (2020 RHDAustralia guideline).

stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur identified at preschool entry assessment. He was born at term by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery with a birthweight of 3.2 kg, but his height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on the 10th centile, despite a mid-parental height on the 50th centile. His developmental milestones have been mildly delayed: he sat unsupported at 9 months, walked unaided at 18 months, and now uses only 2 to 3 word phrases at age 3. He has not had any significant illnesses and has been fully immunised. On examination he has hypertelorism with downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck with low posterior hairline, and a pectus carinatum superiorly with pectus excavatum inferiorly. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge, loudest on inspiration, with a soft single S2 and no thrill; the murmur does not radiate to the carotids. There is no left ventricular heave and the apex is not displaced. Blood pressure is symmetric in both arms at 96/58 mmHg and femoral pulses are present and equal. Echocardiogram performed by the paediatric cardiology team reveals a thickened, dysplastic pulmonary valve with a peak gradient of 45 mmHg, mild post-stenotic dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, and no left ventricular hypertrophy. Genetic testing for RASopathy genes via a multi-gene panel is pending.
lead_in: Which single genetic syndrome best fits this clinical picture?

options.text: Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion)
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis with a right-upper sternal edge murmur and elfin facies; this child has pulmonary stenosis and Noonan facies, per BMJ Best Practice: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Turner syndrome (45,X)
options.rationale: Turner syndrome occurs in phenotypic females with bicuspid AV and coarctation; this patient is male with pulmonary stenosis, per BMJ Best Practice: Turner syndrome.

options.text: Noonan syndrome (PTPN11 mutation)
options.rationale: Noonan syndrome features dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis (most common cardiac lesion in approximately 50%, with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in around 20%), short stature, hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, webbed neck, pectus deformity, and developmental delay; PTPN11 mutations account for about half of cases, per BMJ Best Practice: Noonan syndrome and RCH CPG.

options.text: Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation)
options.rationale: Marfan features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation, not short stature, webbed neck or pulmonary stenosis, per the revised Ghent criteria.

options.text: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)
options.rationale: T21 features upslanting fissures, brachycephaly, single palmar crease and AVSD, not pulmonary stenosis or downslanting fissures, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

stem: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric cardiology clinic by his GP because of a tall, thin habitus and a recently identified murmur during a sports pre-participation examination. He has a height on the 99th centile and an arm span 7 cm greater than his height, with a reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio. He has long, slender fingers with positive Steinberg (thumb) and Walker-Murdoch (wrist) signs bilaterally. There is significant pectus excavatum requiring surgical consideration, a high-arched palate with dental crowding requiring orthodontic intervention, and mild scoliosis with Cobb angle 22 degrees on standing radiograph. An ophthalmology slit-lamp examination performed last month demonstrates bilateral superior ectopia lentis with mild myopia. Echocardiogram performed today shows aortic root dilatation at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.4 cm, corresponding to a Z-score of 3.8 indexed to body surface area, with mild aortic regurgitation and no evidence of dissection. The remaining cardiac anatomy is normal. His father died suddenly aged 38 from a presumed dissection of the ascending aorta while playing tennis; no formal post-mortem genetic testing was performed at the time. He currently plays competitive rugby league at school and trains four times per week with intensive resistance work. There is no history of joint dislocation or skin hyperextensibility. He has no learning difficulties, no recent syncope, and no exertional chest pain.
lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure as athletic build, continue rugby league, and arrange repeat echo in 5 years
options.rationale: He meets revised Ghent criteria for Marfan syndrome (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score >= 2 with family history is more than sufficient); reassurance and continuation of high-impact contact sport is unsafe, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily and clear him to play competitive contact sport
options.rationale: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan syndrome; beta-blocker (atenolol or propranolol) or losartan is used to slow aortic dilatation, and contact and competitive sport should be avoided, per AHA scientific statement on aortopathies.

options.text: Refer for immediate elective ascending aortic root replacement irrespective of Z-score
options.rationale: Elective root replacement is generally indicated at root diameter >= 5.0 cm in adults or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection or rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with a Z-score of 3.8 medical therapy and ongoing surveillance are first-line, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Atenolol or losartan, restrict competitive sport, annual echo and ophthalmology, FBN1 cascade screening
options.rationale: Marfan syndrome with aortic root dilatation requires medical therapy to slow root growth (beta-blocker or losartan, with evidence supporting both), restriction from competitive contact and isometric sport, annual echo surveillance, ophthalmology review for ectopia lentis and retinal complications, and cascade screening of first-degree relatives with FBN1 testing, per BMJ Best Practice and revised Ghent nosology.

options.text: Treat with ramipril 5 mg daily and clear him for non-contact competitive swimming
options.rationale: Losartan (angiotensin receptor blocker), not ramipril, is the agent shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is also restricted, per BMJ Best Practice.

stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary developmental paediatrician for assessment of intellectual disability and behavioural difficulties that have escalated over the past school year. His mother reports global developmental delay first noted at 18 months of age when he had only 2 words and was unable to follow simple instructions. He now attends a mainstream school with full-time aide support but has a recent Wechsler full-scale IQ of 58, placing him in the moderate intellectual disability range. He has marked attentional difficulties consistent with comorbid ADHD, hand-flapping when excited, persistently poor eye contact, perseverative speech with echolalia, and tactile hypersensitivity to clothing labels and certain food textures. He has had no seizures and has not lost previously acquired skills. On examination today he has a long narrow face with prominent forehead, large protruding ears, a high-arched palate, hyperextensible finger joints with thumbs that easily reach the volar forearm, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, and bilateral pes planus. Testicular examination shows symmetrically enlarged testes for prepubertal age, already at a Prader orchidometer volume of 6 mL bilaterally. There is no microcephaly, no neurocutaneous stigmata, and no dysmorphic facial midline defects. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with mild intellectual disability and progressive ataxia who was diagnosed in his 60s with a late-onset intention tremor and gait disorder of unclear aetiology, and a maternal grandmother who experienced primary ovarian insufficiency with cessation of menses at age 36. Microarray comparative genomic hybridisation performed at age 4 was normal, as was a metabolic screen including very long chain fatty acids. The clinical geneticist is now considering targeted single-gene testing based on the clinical pattern.
lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely genetic diagnosis and the appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY); send a peripheral blood karyotype with chromosome enumeration
options.rationale: Klinefelter syndrome presents with tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism), gynaecomastia in adolescence, and milder cognitive impact, per BMJ Best Practice: Klinefelter syndrome.

options.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for 7q11.23 elastin deletion
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features elfin facies, supravalvular AS, hypercalcaemia and an overfriendly personality; family history and macroorchidism do not fit, per RCH CPG: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; request DNA methylation analysis at the 15q11-q13 locus
options.rationale: Prader-Willi presents with neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia in childhood, obesity, hypogonadism (small testes), and short stature, with no macroorchidism or X-linked family pattern, per BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; request TSC1 and TSC2 gene sequencing
options.rationale: TSC presents with hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas, renal angiomyolipomas, cortical tubers and epilepsy; none of these features are present, per BMJ Best Practice: Tuberous sclerosis.

options.text: Fragile X (FMR1 CGG expansion >200); request targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation
options.rationale: The combination of moderate intellectual disability, autistic features, long face with prominent ears, joint hypermobility, post-pubertal macroorchidism (here pre-pubertal but already noted), a maternal uncle with FXTAS (fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome from a premutation) and maternal grandmother with FXPOI (fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency) strongly indicates Fragile X (FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG); microarrays do not detect trinucleotide expansions and FMR1 targeted testing with methylation analysis is required, per RCH CPG: Fragile X and BMJ Best Practice.

stem: A 9-year-old boy weighing 30 kg is 6 hours into treatment of newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis in a paediatric high dependency unit at a tertiary children's hospital. His initial pH was 7.02 with HCO3 5 mmol/L, glucose 38 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L (corrected sodium 142 mmol/L), potassium 4.6 mmol/L and chloride 100 mmol/L. He received a single 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then maintenance fluid plus 48-hour deficit replacement using 0.9% saline with potassium chloride 40 mmol/L, and an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 90 minutes after fluids commenced. He has not received any sodium bicarbonate. Glucose is now 16 mmol/L, pH 7.18 and HCO3 11 mmol/L, and the nurses have just transitioned the fluid to 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose. Over the last 30 minutes the bedside nurse has noted progressively increasing irritability, a new complaint of headache, and now a single episode of non-bilious vomiting. On urgent reassessment his GCS has fallen from 15 to 11 (E3 V3 M5), with a heart rate that has slowed from 120 to 78 bpm and a rising blood pressure of 138/92 mmHg. Pupils are 4 mm and sluggish bilaterally with no focal motor deficit and no posturing. The bedside glucose is 14 mmol/L and the most recent potassium was 3.9 mmol/L. The consultant intensivist is en route from the ICU upstairs, the airway trolley with appropriately sized equipment is at the bedside, and 3% saline and mannitol are available in the resus bay.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to twice maintenance and rebolus 10 mL/kg of 0.9% saline to address suspected hypovolaemia
options.rationale: Bradycardia with hypertension and falling GCS is the Cushing response indicating raised intracranial pressure from cerebral oedema; fluid expansion worsens cerebral oedema, and the fluid rate must be reduced not increased per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion and give an IV bolus of 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose is 14 mmol/L and is not the cause of declining GCS; stopping insulin worsens ketosis, and the clinical picture points to cerebral oedema not hypoglycaemia per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg IV over 15 minutes, reduce IV fluid rate by a third, urgent CT brain
options.rationale: Suspected DKA-associated cerebral oedema (declining GCS, headache, Cushing response, vomiting) is treated immediately with hypertonic saline 2.7-3% at 2.5-5 mL/kg over 10-15 minutes (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduction of IV fluid rate by one third, head-up 30 degrees, and neuroimaging only after stabilisation per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and ISPAD 2022.

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg infused over 30 minutes to correct the residual acidosis safely
options.rationale: Bicarbonate administration is an independent risk factor for DKA-associated cerebral oedema and is not indicated; pH is already improving to 7.18 per ISPAD 2022.

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis as cause of the altered consciousness today
options.rationale: LP in raised intracranial pressure risks coning and would delay osmotherapy; the clinical picture (Cushing response in established DKA) is cerebral oedema per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.
